chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Computer Graphics. (3-0). Credit 3. Representation of 3-dimensional objects, including polyhedral objects, curved surfaces, volumetric representations and CSG models; techniques for hidden surface/edge removal and volume rendering; illumination and shading; antialiasing; ray tracing; radiosity; animation; practical experience with state-of-the-art graphics hardware and software. Prerequisite: CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross-listed with VIZA 672.

chunks.title: Course overview
chunks.content: This course provides an in-depth introduction to physically based rendering, with an emphasis on Monte Carlo techniques. We will study how light interacts with objects in the real world, how image formation is mathematically formulated, and how these formulations are solved in practice using modern rendering algorithms. The course focuses on the theoretical foundations of rendering as well as their application in practical image synthesis systems.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross Listing: VIZA 672/CSCE 641.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Understand the physical and mathematical principles underlying light transport, image formation, and physically based rendering, including
• radiometric quantities and the rendering equation.
• Implement core rendering algorithms such as ray tracing, direct illumination, and global illumination methods.
• Analyze the sources of bias and variance in Monte Carlo rendering algorithms and evaluate the impact of different sampling and acceleration strategies on rendering quality and performance.
• Design and implement a physically based rendering system or rendering component that integrates multiple techniques covered in the course.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation (4th edition)
Authors: Matt Pharr, Wenzel Jakob, and Greg Humphreys
URL for Resource: https://pbr-book.org/4ed/contents

This material Is: Recommended
Robust Monte Carlo Methods For Light Transport Simulation
Authors: Eric Veach
URL for Resource:
https://graphics.stanford.edu/papers/veach_thesis/thesis.pdf

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Programming assignments (70%): There will be 4 programming assignments in C++. One of the assignments is just setting up the environment and finishing a simple task. So there is only 3 main assignments in practice.

Final project (30%): Students need to implement additional rendering features not covered in the assignments. They can work individually or in teams of up to 3 students. Students will propose their project at the middle of the semester. The final project report will be due on April 30th.

Grading Scale: A = 90-100, B < 90 and >= 80, C < 80 and >= 70, D < 70 and >= 60, F < 60

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule Z).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: You will lose 20% from each assignment for each day that it is late. However, there will be 5 granted late days for the entire course. You are free to use it for any of the assignments (note that, you CANNOT use it for the final project!). You will not get any bonuses for any of the unused late days. All the assignments are due at 11:59 pm on Canvas unless otherwise stated. Note that, one minute over and 23 hours over both count as one full day.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
15 weeks course schedule containing the topics for every week and the due dates for the assignments.

| Week | Topics | Assignments |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Introduction and Linear Algebra | |
| 2 | Ray Tracing | HW 0 out |
| 3-4 | Acceleration and Shading | HW 0 due |
| 5 | Texturing Methods | |
| 6 | Physics of Light | |
| 7-8 | Monte Carlo Integration and Sampling | HW 1 out |
| 9-10 | Direct and Global Illumination | HW 1 due & HW 2 out |
| 11 | Microfacet Models | HW 2 due & HW 3 out |
| 12-13 | Volume Rendering | HW 3 due |
| 14 | Advanced Topics | |
| 15 | Finals | Final project due |

chunks.title: Academic Integrity
chunks.content: The assignments in this class are individual unless otherwise stated. For the individual assignments, all code must be written by the student. If specified in the assignment instructions, the use of external libraries for performing basic operations is allowed. However, using any external source code is NOT permitted. Collaborating with other students beyond general discussion is also NOT allowed. In particular, viewing other students' code and/or written answers is strictly prohibited. Posting your code on publicly accessible websites (e.g., GitHub), even after the assignment deadline, is also NOT allowed.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
The use of large language models such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or Claude is not strictly prohibited. However, these tools may be used only for debugging or clarification purposes, not for generating code. You are expected to write all code yourself, and copying or modifying Al-generated code is not permitted. If you use any Al tool in any capacity while completing an assignment, even to ask a conceptual question, you must submit a transcript of the full interaction along with your work.

The instructor may request a meeting with any student to discuss their code and assess their understanding. If a student is unable to adequately explain their own code, it will be considered an honor code violation and reported to the honor council.
